Name: simpsons-paradox
Description: Digital mirror of the Simpson's Paradox domain. The entities are Studies, Treatments, Strata, and CaseCells (raw patient counts). SuccessRates, TreatmentSummaries, and TreatmentRankings are derived. The paradox falls out of the DAG as an emergent derived fact — it is never modeled directly.

Loops.Description: Table: Loops — the 10-loop build plan, carried as a first-class entity inside the model. Each row documents what new concept is introduced and why.

schema.name: LoopId
schema.Description: Unique identifier: loop-01 through loop-10.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Short title for what this loop adds to the domain model.

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: planned | in-progress | complete

schema.name: NewConcept
schema.Description: The new first-class domain concept introduced this loop.

schema.name: DomainQuestion
schema.Description: The natural-language question this loop's concept answers FROM the domain data.

schema.name: MockDataNote
schema.Description: What the seed data is designed to show.

schema.name: NextSuggestion
schema.Description: Suggestion for the next loop, written after witnessing the DAG at this loop's end.

data.Title: Core domain: Study, Treatment, Stratum, CaseCell → SuccessRate falls out
data.NewConcept: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells
data.DomainQuestion: For each (stratum, treatment) cell in a study, what is the success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney stone study (Charig 1986): 2 strata (small/large), 2 treatments (A/B), 4 cells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment, what is its pooled (across all strata) success rate? That requires TreatmentSummary — aggregating CaseCells by treatment, ignoring stratum.

data.Title: TreatmentSummary: pooled rate per treatment across all strata
data.NewConcept: TreatmentSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: If you ignore stratum entirely, what is each treatment's overall success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled: A=273/350=78%, B=289/350=83%. B looks better — this is the misleading aggregate view.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each (stratum, treatment) pair, what is the success rate WITHIN that stratum? That requires StratumSummary — the view that contradicts the pooled view.

data.Title: StratumSummary: per-stratum rate per treatment — the true picture
data.NewConcept: StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: Within each stratum, which treatment has the higher success rate?
data.MockDataNote: Small: A=93.1% > B=86.7%. Large: A=73.0% > B=68.8%. A wins in BOTH strata. Contradiction with pooled view is now visible.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: for each treatment pair in a study, does the pooled winner match the per-stratum winner? That requires TreatmentRanking — the node where the paradox either exists or doesn't.

data.Title: TreatmentRanking: does the pooled winner = the per-stratum winner?
data.NewConcept: TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: When pooled and stratified conclusions disagree — which treatment should actually be preferred?
data.MockDataNote: Pooled winner = B. Per-stratum winner = A in every stratum. Simpson's Paradox: IsReversal = true. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: WHY does the paradox occur? The stratifying variable (stone size) affects both treatment assignment AND outcome. Name it as a StratumVariable with a CausalRole field.

data.Title: StratumVariable: name the confounding factor, its causal role, and derive IsConfounder + IsParadoxExplained
data.NewConcept: StratumVariables (one per study); CausalRole field; IsConfounder boolean; IsParadoxExplained on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: What IS the stratum variable, why does it confound, and does the model witness its own explanation?
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: VariableName=stone_size, CausalRole=confounder, AffectsTreatmentAssignment=true, AffectsOutcome=true → IsConfounder=true → IsParadoxExplained=true on TreatmentRankings.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how large is the allocation imbalance? Derive TreatmentExposureFraction on CaseCells — the fraction of each treatment's cases that land in each stratum. High imbalance = confounding mechanism as a witnessed number.

data.Title: TreatmentExposureFraction: the allocation imbalance as a witnessed number
data.NewConcept: TreatmentExposureFraction field on CaseCells (Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment in this study)
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of each treatment's total cases fall in each stratum? High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding.
data.MockDataNote: A: 263/350=75% in large-stone stratum. B: 80/350=23% in large-stone stratum. The 52-point gap IS the mechanism — now a first-class derived number.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a degenerate study where treatment allocation is perfectly balanced across strata. IsReversal must be FALSE — not because the data says so, but because the algebra guarantees it. The model witnesses its own structural impossibility.

data.Title: Degenerate study: balanced allocation → IsReversal structurally impossible
data.NewConcept: Second Studies row (balanced-synthetic); equal TreatmentExposureFraction across strata; IsReversal = FALSE by construction
data.DomainQuestion: If every stratum gets the same treatment-allocation fraction, can a reversal occur? The DAG should witness that it cannot.
data.MockDataNote: Synthetic: A and B each assigned 50/50 to small/large strata. A=60% success both strata, B=50% both. Pooled: A=60%, B=50%. No reversal. IsParadoxExplained vacuously true (IsConfounder=false, no confounding, no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: Add Berkeley 1973 — same DAG, contested domain. Aggregate: women admitted at lower rate. Per-department: women admitted at equal or higher rate in most. IsReversal=true. But IsConfounder is ambiguous — department choice may be downstream of gender (mediator, not confounder). The model witnesses its own explanatory limits.

data.Title: Berkeley 1973: contested confounder — model reveals a PARTIAL paradox, not a clean reversal
data.NewConcept: Third Studies row (berkeley-1973); StratumVariable=department; CausalRole=contested; IsConfounder=false; IsParadoxExplained=false; PerStratumWinner=none (mixed, not unanimous)
data.DomainQuestion: Does Berkeley show a clean reversal? No — PerStratumWinner='none' because the per-stratum winner is mixed (female wins 3/4 depts, male wins 1). The model surfaces a structural limit of the reversal definition: partial paradoxes are real but don't satisfy the strict IsReversal criterion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed: male pooled 52.8% vs female 39.9% (large gap). Per dept: female wins A (82%>62%), B (68%>63%), D (35%>33%); male wins C (37%>34%). No unanimous per-stratum winner → IsReversal=FALSE by strict definition. The intuitive paradox is real; the formal paradox is partial.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ParadoxStrength (scalar severity) to capture partial reversals — pooled gap and average per-stratum gap regardless of unanimity. Then StudySummary for epistemic coverage across all 3 studies.

data.Title: ParadoxStrength: scalar severity derived from pooled gap and per-stratum gap
data.NewConcept: ParadoxStrength on TreatmentRankings; PooledGap and AvgStratumGap on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: How large is the reversal? Can we rank studies by paradox severity, and does strength correlate with allocation imbalance?
data.MockDataNote: Kidney: pooled gap ~4.6pp favoring B, avg stratum gap ~5pp favoring A. Berkeley: larger pooled gap, varies by department. Balanced: strength=0 (no reversal).
data.NextSuggestion: StudySummary: roll up ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, AvgParadoxStrength across all studies. The model now describes its own epistemic coverage in a single view.

data.Title: StudySummary: the model witnesses its own epistemic coverage
data.NewConcept: StudySummary table (one row per model instance): StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, UnexplainedCount, ImpossibleCount, AvgParadoxStrength
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the epistemic state? How many reversals, how many explained, how many witnessed-but-unexplained?
data.MockDataNote: 3 studies: ReversalCount=2 (kidney+berkeley), ExplainedCount=1 (kidney), UnexplainedCount=1 (berkeley), ImpossibleCount=1 (balanced). The model now holds a mirror to itself.
data.NextSuggestion: Ask: how heavy is each stratum? Add StratumFraction to StratumSummaries — the fraction of the study's total cases that land in each stratum. The weight each stratum carries IS the lever the confounder pulls.

data.Title: StratumFraction: the weight each stratum carries in the pooled average
data.NewConcept: StratumFraction (and StudyTotalCases, StratumTotalCases) on StratumSummaries
data.DomainQuestion: What fraction of the study's total cases land in each stratum? High StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down — and when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, the reversal follows.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: large-stratum = (263+80)/700 = 49%; small-stratum = (87+270)/700 = 51%. Nearly equal stratum weights — but unequal per-treatment allocation (75% of A is in large; 23% of B is in large). The weight alone is not the mechanism; the differential allocation into heavy strata is.
data.NextSuggestion: Add WeightedStratumRate: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate per treatment row. Sum these across strata to reconstruct PooledRate from first principles. The DAG now witnesses the mechanism equation: pooled rate = weighted average of stratum rates.

data.Title: WeightedPull: mechanism equation witnessed — pooled rate = weighted sum of stratum rates
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumRate on StratumSummaries; PooledRateFromWeightsA and PooledRateFromWeightsB on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: Can the DAG reconstruct each treatment's pooled rate purely from stratum weights × stratum rates? If PooledRateFromWeights = PooledRate, the mechanism equation is verified: Simpson's Paradox is algebraically explained.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: PooledRateFromWeightsA=0.833, PooledRateFromWeightsB=0.779 (A looks better under equal study-level weights). But actual pooled rates are A=0.780, B=0.826 (B looks better). The divergence IS the mechanism: real pooled uses treatment-level allocation weights; the reconstruction uses study-level weights. The swap between the two orderings shows exactly how differential allocation flips the conclusion. balanced-synthetic: weights match exactly (equal allocation = no flip possible).
data.NextSuggestion: Add AllocationBias to StratumSummaries: the difference between TreatmentExposureFraction for A and B in each stratum. High bias toward the losing treatment in a low-success stratum IS the confounder's fingerprint as a witnessed number.

data.Title: AllocationBias: the confounder's fingerprint as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: AllocationBias on StratumSummaries (AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB); also AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, TreatmentACasesHere, TreatmentBCasesHere, TreatmentATotalCases, TreatmentBTotalCases
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, is one treatment disproportionately concentrated here? AllocationBias = AllocationFractionA - AllocationFractionB: positive means A is over-represented here, negative means B is. A strong negative bias in a low-success stratum is the confounder's fingerprint.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986 large-stratum: AllocationFractionA=263/350=0.751, AllocationFractionB=80/350=0.229, AllocationBias=+0.523. A is massively over-allocated to the hard (large-stone) stratum. This single number summarizes why the pooled rate misleads: A carries 52pp more exposure to the hard case, dragging its pooled average down below B's despite beating B in both strata.
data.NextSuggestion: Add a 4th study: kidney-balanced — same stratum rates as kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (50/50 split). AllocationBias=0 everywhere, IsReversal=FALSE. The counterfactual shows the reversal is purely an allocation artifact, not a property of the treatment rates themselves.

data.Title: Counterfactual: kidney-balanced isolates allocation as the sole cause of the reversal
data.NewConcept: Fourth study (kidney-balanced): same stratum rates as kidney-1986, equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationBias=0, ParadoxStrength=0.
data.DomainQuestion: If the same doctors, same disease, same treatments, same stratum difficulty — but patients were allocated equally — would the paradox still exist? The DAG answers: no. The reversal lives in the allocation, not in the treatment rates.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-balanced: PooledRateA = (163+128)/350 = 0.831, PooledRateB = (152+120)/350 = 0.777. A wins pooled AND per-stratum. IsReversal=FALSE, ParadoxStrength=0. AllocationBias=0 in both strata. The counterfactual witnesses that the reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an artifact of differential allocation, not of treatment efficacy.
data.NextSuggestion: Add ReversalThreshold to TreatmentRankings: the minimum AllocationBias magnitude needed to flip the pooled winner. How far is kidney-1986 from the threshold? How close is berkeley-1973? This quantifies paradox fragility vs robustness.

data.Title: ReversalThreshold: how robust or fragile is each study's paradox?
data.NewConcept: MaxAllocationBias, WeightedStratumGapSum, ThresholdMargin on TreatmentRankings
data.DomainQuestion: How much allocation bias is needed to produce a reversal? Is the observed bias well above threshold (robust paradox) or barely over it (fragile paradox)? ThresholdMargin = observed bias minus minimum needed.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: ReversalIntensity=1.0, ThresholdMargin=+0.5 — maximally robust, every stratum opposes pooled. berkeley-1973: ReversalIntensity=0.75, ThresholdMargin=+0.25 — past threshold but not unanimous (3/4 departments oppose). balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced: intensity=0, margin=-0.5 — structurally impossible. ThresholdMargin > 0 means the paradox is robust; < 0 means absent or impossible.
data.NextSuggestion: Pour a real-world dataset with a known mediator (not confounder) through the same DAG and witness where it lands on the reversal_intensity × paradox_strength plane. The geometry of the plane is the next frontier.

data.Title: StratumGap: per-stratum rate difference as a signed witnessed number
data.NewConcept: StratumGap field on StratumSummaries (StratumRateA - StratumRateB, signed)
data.DomainQuestion: In each stratum, how large is the treatment advantage, and in which direction? StratumGap > 0 means A wins here; < 0 means B wins. Summing StratumGap × StratumFraction gives the equal-weight pooled gap — a new derived node.
data.MockDataNote: kidney-1986: small-stratum gap = +0.064 (A wins by 6.4pp), large-stratum gap = +0.043 (A wins by 4.3pp). Both positive — A wins everywhere. The pooled gap is negative (B appears to win by 4.6pp) purely due to allocation. The signed gap makes this contradiction arithmetically visible in one column. kidney-balanced: small=+0.064, large=+0.042 (same signs, same magnitudes — the rates are identical to kidney-1986 but now A also wins pooled because allocation is balanced). balanced-synthetic: small=+0.10, large=+0.10 (constant, equal allocation). berkeley-1973: dept-A=−0.200 (female wins), dept-B=+0.011 (male wins barely), dept-C=−0.002 (female wins barely), dept-D=−0.016 (female wins). Mixed signs confirm the partial paradox: no unanimous StratumGap direction, hence no clean reversal.
data.NextSuggestion: Derive WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings: SUM(StratumGap × StratumFraction) aggregated across all TreatmentA rows for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap allocation-free weighting would produce. When WeightedStratumGapSum and PooledGap have opposite signs, the allocation has not just compressed the signal but flipped it. That sign flip is a more precise characterisation of Simpson's Paradox than IsReversal: it handles partial flips and gives a magnitude to the distortion.

data.Title: WeightedStratumGapSum: the equal-weight signal — and the Berkeley category error witnessed
data.NewConcept: WeightedStratumGap on StratumSummaries (StratumGap × StratumFraction, the per-stratum contribution to the equal-weight pooled gap); WeightedStratumGapSum on TreatmentRankings (SUMIFS of WeightedStratumGap across TreatmentA rows); SignedPooledGap (PooledRateA − PooledRateB, signed); IsSignFlip boolean (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs)
data.DomainQuestion: If allocation were equal across strata, what would the pooled gap be? WeightedStratumGapSum answers this. When it has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not just compressed the signal — it has flipped it. That sign flip IS Simpson's Paradox under a continuous definition. Does Berkeley sign-flip?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB: balanced-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.1000 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, no distortion). kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457 → IsSignFlip=TRUE (A wins in every stratum by 5.4pp on average, but actual pooled shows B winning by 4.6pp). kidney-balanced: WSGSUM=+0.0543, SignedPooledGap=+0.0543 → IsSignFlip=FALSE (equal allocation, WSGSUM = actual pooled gap). berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292 → IsSignFlip=TRUE. Per-stratum breakdown: dept-A gap=−0.2035 (female wins by 20.4pp, fraction=0.289); dept-B=−0.0496 (female wins, fraction=0.181); dept-C=+0.0286 (male wins barely, fraction=0.284); dept-D=−0.0184 (female wins, fraction=0.245). Under equal allocation female would win pooled by 6.4pp — but the actual pooled shows male winning by 12.9pp. Berkeley IS a Simpson's Paradox under the sign-flip definition despite IsReversal=FALSE. The binary unanimity criterion excluded Berkeley because dept-C goes to male; the sign-flip criterion correctly includes it.
data.NextSuggestion: The sign flip is witnessed. Now measure the magnitude: how far has the allocation bent the pooled signal? AllocationDistortion = |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Berkeley: |−0.0642 − (+0.1292)| = 0.1934. Kidney: |+0.0537 − (−0.0457)| = 0.0994. Berkeley's distortion is nearly twice kidney's — despite IsReversal=FALSE. This is the single-number measure that ranks studies by how badly confounding has distorted their pooled result.

data.Title: AllocationDistortion: the magnitude of the signal the allocation erased or fabricated
data.NewConcept: AllocationDistortion on TreatmentRankings (|WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|) — the scalar distance between the equal-weight signal and the actual pooled signal
data.DomainQuestion: How much has the allocation distorted the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show? AllocationDistortion=0 means the allocation is neutral. A large AllocationDistortion means the pooled number is mostly noise from case assignment, not signal about treatment efficacy.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, AllocationDistortion=0.1934. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, AllocationDistortion=0.0994. kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (balanced allocation, no distortion). balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 (equal allocation, no distortion). The allocation is injecting a 19.3pp distortion into Berkeley's pooled result — reversing a real female advantage of 6.4pp into an apparent male advantage of 12.9pp. This is nearly twice the 9.9pp distortion in kidney-1986. ParadoxStrength (old measure) gives berkeley=0.0969, kidney=0.0457 — same ordinal ranking but ParadoxStrength is penalized by the unanimity criterion: Berkeley's 0.75 ReversalIntensity reduces its score. AllocationDistortion is not so penalized: it measures what the allocation DID to the signal, not whether the outcome passes an arbitrary threshold.
data.NextSuggestion: Two severity measures are now live: ParadoxStrength and AllocationDistortion. They agree on ordinal ranking across our four studies but diverge in magnitude (Berkeley is 2× kidney under AllocationDistortion but only 2.1× under ParadoxStrength). The more important question: is a study with AllocationDistortion > 0 but IsSignFlip=FALSE possible? That would be a type-C study — allocation has compressed but not reversed the signal. None of the current studies are type-C. Design a synthetic type-C study: same-sign WSGSUM and SignedPooledGap, but WSGSUM closer to zero than SignedPooledGap. Add it to the data and witness AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE.

data.Title: Type-C witness: compressed-synthetic — allocation distortion without sign flip
data.NewConcept: compressed-synthetic study (fifth study): type-C case — A wins every stratum and wins pooled, but allocation compresses A's pooled advantage. Witnesses AllocationDistortion > 0, IsSignFlip=FALSE, IsReversal=FALSE, PerStratumWinner=A. Completes the four-type taxonomy.
data.DomainQuestion: Does a study with real allocation distortion (AllocationDistortion > 0) but no sign flip (IsSignFlip=FALSE) exist? If yes, the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy is nonempty — there is a class of misleading aggregates that compress but do not reverse the pooled signal. This class is invisible to both IsReversal and IsSignFlip but is visible to AllocationDistortion.
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.1934, ReversalIntensity=0.75 → type B. kidney-1986: WSGSUM=+0.0537, SignedPooledGap=−0.0457, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.0994, ReversalIntensity=1.0 → type A. compressed-synthetic: WSGSUM=+0.1000, SignedPooledGap=+0.0600, IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0.0400, ReversalIntensity=0.0 → type C (A wins pooled by 6pp but would win by 10pp under equal allocation). kidney-balanced: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. balanced-synthetic: AllocationDistortion=0.0000 → type D. All four types are now empirically populated. Type-C is important: a researcher seeing pooled A=78%, B=72% and concluding 'A wins by 6pp' is correct — but the equal-weight truth is 'A wins by 10pp.' The allocation has consumed 4pp of signal without a single stratum switching winner.
data.NextSuggestion: All four DistortionType cells are now populated. Add DistortionType as a derived field on TreatmentRankings: A = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity=1.0; B = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity<1.0; C = NOT IsSignFlip AND AllocationDistortion>0.01; D = AllocationDistortion≤0.01. This makes the geometric taxonomy a first-class derived fact in the DAG. Then add PolicyImplication per type: type A = stratify-immediately; type B = investigate-confounder; type C = check-allocation-bias; type D = pooled-analysis-trustworthy.

data.Title: Hydration contract: real study data enters through 05b-customize-data.sql, not the rulebook JSON
data.NewConcept: Real study data lives directly in the rulebook JSON — the SSoT is unified. Provenance files in data/raw/. The view chain computes everything from there.
data.DomainQuestion: Can real published study data enter the instrument through a SQL hydration file (not the rulebook JSON) and produce DistortionType / AllocationDistortion / PolicyImplication results from the existing view chain without any schema changes?
data.MockDataNote: Two real studies hydrated: reintjes-2000 (Epidemiology 2000, N=3519, antibiotic prophylaxis UTI study) and radelet-1981 (Am Soc Rev 1981, N=326, Florida death penalty). Both are type-A full reversals. Witnessed: reintjes AllocationDistortion=0.0516, radelet AllocationDistortion=0.0684. No schema changes were needed. 05b-customize-data.sql is the durable hydration path.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts only synthetic studies in its ReversalCount/NonReversalCount. Add DistortionType aggregations (TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion) so the model's self-portrait reflects all seven studies including the two real ones.

data.Title: Two real studies witnessed: reintjes-2000 and radelet-1981 — both type-A full reversals from real data in the rulebook
data.NewConcept: Two non-canonical published datasets poured through the hydration contract. Provenance files in data/raw/. Instrument produces correct DistortionType=A and PolicyImplication=stratify-immediately for both.
data.DomainQuestion: Do two real published studies with well-known outcomes (antibiotic prophylaxis UTI reversal; racial death-penalty reversal) produce the expected DistortionType=A when hydrated through the instrument? Does the AllocationDistortion magnitude match the known mechanism story?
data.MockDataNote: Witnessed from DB (full table ordered by AllocationDistortion desc): berkeley-1973: type B, distortion=0.1934. kidney-1986: type A, distortion=0.0994. radelet-1981: type A, distortion=0.0684 (REAL). reintjes-2000: type A, distortion=0.0516 (REAL). compressed-synthetic: type C, distortion=0.0400. kidney-balanced: type D, distortion=0.0000. balanced-synthetic: type D, distortion=0.0000. Both real studies are full type-A reversals as expected from the literature. radelet has higher AllocationDistortion (0.0684) than reintjes (0.0516), consistent with the victim-race confound being stronger — black defendants are 62% in the low-sentence black-victim stratum, a more extreme allocation imbalance than reintjes where 87% of prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts only the synthetic study set. Add DistortionType aggregations (TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion) so the model describes its real empirical coverage, not just the synthetic controls.

data.Title: DistortionType + PolicyImplication: the four-type taxonomy as a first-class DAG fact
data.NewConcept: DistortionType (derived field on TreatmentRankings): A = full sign-flip unanimous reversal; B = sign-flip non-unanimous; C = distortion without sign flip; D = neutral. PolicyImplication (derived from DistortionType): A → stratify-immediately; B → investigate-confounder; C → check-allocation-bias; D → pooled-analysis-trustworthy.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the geometric taxonomy (four cells defined by IsSignFlip × ReversalIntensity × AllocationDistortion magnitude) be collapsed into a single derived field that a researcher or policy layer can read without knowing the internal coordinates?
data.MockDataNote: All five current studies resolve cleanly: kidney-1986 → type A (stratify-immediately); berkeley-1973 → type B (investigate-confounder); compressed-synthetic → type C (check-allocation-bias); kidney-balanced → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy); balanced-synthetic → type D (pooled-analysis-trustworthy). DistortionType and PolicyImplication are now first-class columns in vw_TreatmentRankings — the visualizer and any downstream consumer reads them directly without re-deriving the geometry.
data.NextSuggestion: ModelSummary currently counts ReversalCount and NonReversalCount using the old IsReversal (unanimity) criterion. Add DistortionType aggregations: TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount (TypeA+TypeB), and AvgAllocationDistortion. These make the model's self-portrait accurate under the new continuous geometry rather than the obsolete binary criterion.

data.Title: ModelSummary DistortionType aggregations: the model's self-portrait updated to the new geometry
data.NewConcept: TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, SignFlipCount, AvgAllocationDistortion on ModelSummary. RealDataOnly filter so synthetic controls can be excluded from empirical summaries.
data.DomainQuestion: Across all studies in this model, what is the DistortionType breakdown? How many sign-flips, what is the average AllocationDistortion? The current ModelSummary reports reversal_count=5 under the old unanimity criterion — this loop makes it accurate under the new continuous geometry.
data.NextSuggestion: With the model's self-portrait corrected, formally name IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip as a first-class field and document that the unanimity IsReversal is now a strict subtype (DistortionType=A only).

data.Title: IsReversal_v2: formally retire the unanimity criterion as the primary definition
data.NewConcept: IsReversal_v2 = IsSignFlip as a named first-class field on TreatmentRankings. DefinitionDelta = IsReversal_v2 XOR IsReversal (identifies studies where the two definitions disagree — currently berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996). StrictReversalSubtype label preserving the unanimity criterion as DistortionType=A.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the sign-flip definition strictly generalize the old unanimity definition across all 10 hydrated studies? Berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996 are the critical cases: IsReversal=FALSE but IsSignFlip=TRUE. DefinitionDelta witnesses exactly which studies the old definition misclassified.
data.NextSuggestion: Find one real published study that hydrates as DistortionType=C (AllocationDistortion>0, IsSignFlip=FALSE) to prove the type-C cell is not merely a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: Cleanup: resolve internal tensions introduced by the v2 definition
data.NewConcept: IsParadoxExplained_v2 on TreatmentRankings. ReversalCountV2, NonReversalCountV2, ExtendedReversalCount, ExplainedCountV2, DistortionOnlyCount on ModelSummary. Resolves four tensions: (1) ReversalCount/NonReversalCount still used old IsReversal; (2) PartialCount conflated Type-B (extended reversal) with Type-C (distortion only); (3) ExplainedCount missed berkeley and appleton despite their having confirmed confounders; (4) ZeroStrengthCount conflated Type-C and Type-D.
data.DomainQuestion: With IsReversal_v2 as the primary definition, does the model's self-portrait (ModelSummary) accurately reflect the v2 geometry? After this loop: ReversalCountV2=7, ExtendedReversalCount=2, ExplainedCountV2=6 (berkeley-1973 remains unexplained — its confounder is contested, not confirmed), DistortionOnlyCount=1.
data.NextSuggestion: Find one real published study that hydrates as DistortionType=C to prove the type-C cell is not merely a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: Type-C real-data witness: allocation distortion without sign flip in published data
data.NewConcept: One real published study hydrating as DistortionType=C: pooled winner directionally correct but margin materially compressed or inflated by differential allocation. Entered through the same 05b-customize-data.sql hydration contract as reintjes-2000 and radelet-1981.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the published literature contain studies where AllocationDistortion>0 but IsSignFlip=FALSE? Candidate domains: surgical volume (senior surgeons get harder cases but still win pooled), teacher effectiveness (experienced teachers assigned lower-performing students), hospital quality (high-volume centers receive sicker patients). The type-C cell currently contains only compressed-synthetic; this loop proves it is not merely a constructed pathology.
data.NextSuggestion: Build a validation set of 5 additional real studies across diverse domains to confirm multi-domain generalization of the four-type taxonomy.

data.Title: Validation set: 5 additional real studies confirm multi-domain generalization
data.NewConcept: 5 additional real published studies hydrated through 05b-customize-data.sql spanning at least 3 domains (medicine, economics/policy, education or social science). Each produces a witnessed DistortionType and AllocationDistortion. ModelSummary updated to reflect the full empirical set.
data.DomainQuestion: Does the four-type taxonomy classify real studies consistently across domains? Do confounders, mediators, and contested variables produce geometrically distinguishable AllocationDistortion patterns? The validation set answers whether the instrument generalizes or is tuned to the canonical examples.
data.NextSuggestion: Formalize the instrument specification as a first-class InstrumentSpec table so another researcher can hydrate a new study and classify it without knowledge of the internal DAG.

data.Title: Instrument specification: adapter contract, five input fields, four output types
data.NewConcept: InstrumentSpec table: one row per required input field, derived coordinate, classification rule, and adapter contract requirement. Covers the full chain from raw CaseCells (study, stratum, treatment, successes, total_cases) through SignedPooledGap, WeightedStratumGapSum, IsSignFlip, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType, and PolicyImplication. Every row machine-verifiable against the hydrated studies.
data.DomainQuestion: Can the full instrument be stated precisely enough that another researcher or script can hydrate a new study and classify it without access to the internal DAG? The spec is the distillation of loops 1–25 into a reusable tool and the foundation for any preprint or methods paper.
data.NextSuggestion: The instrument specification is the terminal deliverable. A preprint or methods paper follows from the spec + the validation set — but that is an output artifact, not a loop.

Studies.Description: Table: Studies — a clinical or empirical study comparing two or more treatments for a condition, with patient cases stratified by a confounding variable. The top-level entity in the domain.

schema.name: StudyId
schema.Description: Unique slug for this study (e.g. kidney-1986, berkeley-1973).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors StudyId.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable title of the study.

schema.name: Source
schema.Description: Citation or description of the original data source.

schema.name: SourceUrl
schema.Description: URL to the original source.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total number of cases (trials) across all CaseCells in this study.

schema.name: CellCount
schema.Description: Number of CaseCell rows for this study.

data.Title: Kidney Stone Treatment Study (Charig et al. 1986)
data.Source: Charig CR et al. Comparison of treatment of renal calculi by open surgery, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy. Br Med J. 1986;292:879.
data.Name: kidney-1986

data.Title: Balanced Synthetic Study (degenerate case — reversal structurally impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that perfectly balanced treatment allocation across strata algebraically prevents Simpson's Paradox. Not based on real data.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic

data.Title: Kidney Stone Counterfactual (balanced allocation — reversal algebraically impossible)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset derived from Charig 1986 kidney stone rates but with equal treatment allocation across strata (175 cases per cell). Demonstrates that the reversal in the original study is purely an allocation artifact.
data.Name: kidney-balanced

data.Title: UC Berkeley Graduate Admissions (Bickel et al. 1975) — contested confounder
data.Source: Bickel PJ, Hammel EA, O'Connell JW. Sex Bias in Graduate Admissions: Data from Berkeley. Science. 1975;187:398-404.
data.Name: berkeley-1973

data.Title: Compressed Synthetic Study (type-C — allocation distortion without sign flip)
data.Source: Synthetic dataset constructed to demonstrate that allocation can distort the pooled signal (AllocationDistortion > 0) without reversing it (IsSignFlip=FALSE). Treatment A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled — but the pooled margin is compressed by differential allocation. This populates the type-C cell in the DistortionType taxonomy.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic

data.Title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis and UTI Rates in Dutch Hospitals (Reintjes et al. 2000)
data.Source: Reintjes R, de Boer A, van Pelt W, Mintjes-de Groot J. Simpson's paradox: an example from hospital epidemiology. Epidemiology. 2000;11(1):81-83. PMID: 10615849.
data.Name: reintjes-2000

data.Title: Racial Characteristics and the Death Penalty in Florida (Radelet 1981)
data.Source: Radelet ML. Racial characteristics and the imposition of the death penalty. American Sociological Review. 1981;46(6):918-927.
data.Name: radelet-1981

data.Title: Batting Averages: Derek Jeter vs David Justice 1995-1997 (Ross 2007)
data.Source: Ross KA. Repeating effects in baseball. College Mathematics Journal. 2007;38(3):205-210. Raw data from Baseball Almanac (official MLB records).
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997

data.Title: Whickham Smoking Cohort — 20-year Mortality by Smoking Status (Appleton et al. 1996)
data.Source: Appleton DR, French JM, Vanderpump MPJ. Ignoring a covariate: An example of Simpson's paradox. The American Statistician. 1996;50(4):340-341. DOI: 10.2307/2684931. Data: Whickham (England) health survey 1972-74, 20-year follow-up.
data.Name: appleton-1996

data.Title: COVID-19 Delta Variant CFR: Vaccinated vs Unvaccinated by Age Group, UK 2021 (PHE Technical Briefing 20)
data.Source: Public Health England. COVID-19 vaccine surveillance report, Technical Briefing 20 (August 2021). Dataset: OpenIntro simpsons_paradox_covid, contributed by Matthew T. Brenneman, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021

data.Title: CABG Surgery Volume and Mortality, New York State (Hannan et al. 1994 — type-C real witness)
data.Source: Hannan EL, Kilburn H Jr, Racz M, Shields E, Chassin MR. Improving the outcomes of coronary artery bypass surgery in New York State. JAMA. 1994;271(10):761-766. Risk-stratification by operative risk class. Numbers approximated from Table 3 (high-volume vs low-volume centers, survival rates by risk stratum).
data.Name: hannan-1994

Treatments.Description: Table: Treatments — the interventions being compared within a study. Each treatment belongs to one study.

schema.name: TreatmentId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A, kidney-1986-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells and comparisons (e.g. A, B).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what this treatment is.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes for this treatment across all strata.

schema.name: PooledSuccessRate
schema.Description: Pooled (across all strata) success rate for this treatment: TotalSuccesses / TotalCases.

data.Description: Open surgery / percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) — the more invasive procedure, used preferentially for large stones.
data.Name: kidney-1986-A

data.Description: Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) — the less invasive procedure, used more for small stones.
data.Name: kidney-1986-B

data.Description: Open surgery / PCNL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:93.1%, large:73.0%) but now allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment A.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-A

data.Description: ESWL — same per-stratum rates as kidney-1986 (small:86.7%, large:68.8%) but allocated equally (50%) to each stratum. Counterfactual treatment B.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-B

data.Description: Synthetic treatment A — 60% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-A

data.Description: Synthetic treatment B — 50% success rate in both strata. Allocated equally (50%) to each stratum.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-B

data.Description: Male applicants (labeled A). Pooled admission rate appears higher than female.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-male

data.Description: Female applicants (labeled B). Pooled admission rate appears lower, but per-department rates are equal or higher in most departments.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-female

data.Description: Treatment A — 70% success rate in hard stratum, 90% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the hard stratum (60% of A cases go to hard). A wins in both strata and also wins pooled, but pooled margin is compressed by the allocation imbalance.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-A

data.Description: Treatment B — 60% success rate in hard stratum, 80% in easy stratum. Over-allocated to the easy stratum (60% of B cases go to easy). B loses in both strata and also loses pooled, but looks better than it would under equal allocation.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-B

data.Description: Antibiotic prophylaxis given. Within every stratum prophylaxis patients have HIGHER UTI rates than controls. Aggregate inverts due to confounding by hospital incidence level.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-A

data.Description: No antibiotic prophylaxis (control). Within every stratum controls have LOWER UTI rates. Aggregate inverts: controls appear to have higher rates pooled.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-B

data.Description: White defendant. In both victim-race strata, white defendants are LESS likely to receive the death penalty than black defendants. Aggregate inverts: white defendants appear more likely overall due to victim-race confounding.
data.Name: radelet-1981-A

data.Description: Black defendant. In both victim-race strata, black defendants are MORE likely to receive the death penalty. Aggregate inverts: black defendants are concentrated in black-victim cases (low death-sentence rate), pulling their aggregate rate below white defendants.
data.Name: radelet-1981-B

data.Description: Derek Jeter (New York Yankees). Higher combined batting average (.300) despite losing to Justice in every individual season. Bulk at-bats in stronger years (1996: 582 AB, 1997: 654 AB) pull his combined average up.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-A

data.Description: David Justice (Atlanta Braves 1995-96, Cleveland Indians 1997). Higher batting average in each individual year (1995: .253, 1996: .321, 1997: .329) but lower combined (.298). Bulk at-bats in his weakest year (1995: 411 AB at .253) drag his combined average down.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-B

data.Description: Smoker at baseline (1972-74). Higher mortality within every age stratum. Pooled mortality appears LOWER (23.9%) than non-smokers (31.4%) — the reversal. Mechanism: smokers were younger on average at baseline, and younger women have lower 20-year mortality regardless of smoking status.
data.Name: appleton-1996-A

data.Description: Non-smoker at baseline. Lower mortality within every age stratum. Pooled mortality appears HIGHER (31.4%) — misleadingly so. Non-smokers were older on average (survival bias: older women who reached the survey more likely to be lifelong non-smokers), concentrating them in high-mortality age strata.
data.Name: appleton-1996-B

data.Description: Vaccinated (includes partially vaccinated). Within every age stratum, vaccinated individuals have a LOWER COVID-19 Delta death rate than unvaccinated. Pooled death rate appears HIGHER (0.41%) than unvaccinated (0.17%) because vaccinated people were overwhelmingly older — 23% of vaccinated are 50+ vs only 2.3% of unvaccinated, and the 50+ group has ~100× higher CFR.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-A

data.Description: Unvaccinated. Within every age stratum, unvaccinated individuals have a HIGHER COVID-19 Delta death rate than vaccinated. Pooled death rate appears LOWER (0.17%) — a statistical artifact of the unvaccinated cohort being overwhelmingly young (97.7% under 50).
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-B

Strata.Description: Table: Strata — the subdivisions of patients within a study by the value of the confounding variable (e.g. stone size). Each stratum belongs to one study.

schema.name: StratumId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small, kidney-1986-large).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Short label used in CaseCells (e.g. small, large, dept-A).

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Plain-language description of what defines this stratum.

schema.name: TotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across all treatments.

data.Description: Patients with small kidney stones — easier to treat, higher baseline success rate regardless of treatment.
data.Name: kidney-1986-small

data.Description: Patients with large kidney stones — harder to treat, lower baseline success rate regardless of treatment. Preferentially assigned to Treatment A.
data.Name: kidney-1986-large

data.Description: Small kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 small stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Counterfactual stratum.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-small

data.Description: Large kidney stones — same baseline rates as kidney-1986 large stratum but now receiving exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-large

data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-easy

data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rate. Receives exactly 50% of each treatment's cases. Equal allocation prevents confounding.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-hard

data.Description: Dept A — highly competitive (low admission rate ~64%). Women applied here at roughly equal rates to men.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-A

data.Description: Dept B — competitive (~35% admission). Women applied here less often.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-B

data.Description: Dept C — moderately selective (~34% admission). More balanced gender application.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-C

data.Description: Dept D — open (~64% admission). Women applied here most heavily — this is the easy department that women disproportionately chose.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-dept-D

data.Description: Hard stratum — lower baseline success rates (A=70%, B=60%). Treatment A is over-allocated here (60% of A cases vs 40% of B cases). This is the stratum that pulls A's pooled rate down relative to equal-weight expectation.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-hard

data.Description: Easy stratum — higher baseline success rates (A=90%, B=80%). Treatment B is over-allocated here (60% of B cases vs 40% of A cases). This partially offsets B's per-stratum disadvantage in the pooled analysis.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-easy

data.Description: Low-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate ≤2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 20/1113=1.80%. Control rate: 5/720=0.69%. Prophylaxis is harmful here. Most prophylaxis patients land in this stratum (87%), pulling the aggregate prophylaxis rate down and fabricating an apparent protective effect pooled.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-low

data.Description: High-incidence hospitals (baseline UTI rate >2.5%). Prophylaxis rate: 22/166=13.25%. Control rate: 99/1520=6.51%. Prophylaxis is again harmful. Controls are heavily concentrated here (1520 of 2240=68%), inflating the aggregate control rate.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-high

data.Description: Cases with a white victim. Death penalty issued far more often here (~17% pooled). White def: 19/151=12.58%. Black def: 11/63=17.46%. Black defendants MORE likely within this stratum.
data.Name: radelet-1981-white-victim

data.Description: Cases with a black victim. Death penalty rare here (~3.3% pooled). White def: 0/9=0.00%. Black def: 6/103=5.83%. Black defendants STILL more likely. Most black defendants are in this low-rate stratum (103/166=62%), dragging their aggregate rate down.
data.Name: radelet-1981-black-victim

data.Description: 1995 season. Jeter: 12/48=.250. Justice: 104/411=.253. Justice wins by 3 points. Small season for Jeter (48 AB, rookie call-up); Justice's heaviest season (411 AB) and his weakest average — this is the stratum doing the most damage to Justice's combined rate.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1995

data.Description: 1996 season. Jeter: 183/582=.314. Justice: 45/140=.321. Justice wins by 7 points. Jeter's breakout full season (582 AB). Justice had a shortened season (140 AB) but a high average — a small, strong stratum that lifts his per-year record but contributes little weight to combined.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1996

data.Description: 1997 season. Jeter: 190/654=.291. Justice: 163/495=.329. Justice wins by 38 points — the largest per-stratum gap. Jeter's largest season (654 AB). The allocation imbalance is clear: Jeter has 654 AB in a .291 year vs Justice's 495 at .329.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-1997

data.Description: Age 18-24 at baseline. Smokers: 2/55=3.6% mortality. Non-smokers: 1/72=1.4%. Smokers worse. Very low mortality overall; small absolute differences.
data.Name: appleton-1996-18-24

data.Description: Age 25-34 at baseline. Smokers: 3/125=2.4% mortality. Non-smokers: 4/156=2.6%. Effectively tied (smokers marginally better — near-zero stratum gap). Very low mortality stratum.
data.Name: appleton-1996-25-34

data.Description: Age 35-44 at baseline. Smokers: 10/105=9.5% mortality. Non-smokers: 7/111=6.3%. Smokers clearly worse.
data.Name: appleton-1996-35-44

data.Description: Age 45-54 at baseline. Smokers: 28/131=21.4% mortality. Non-smokers: 12/78=15.4%. Smokers worse; gap widening with age.
data.Name: appleton-1996-45-54

data.Description: Age 55-64 at baseline. Smokers: 52/116=44.8% mortality. Non-smokers: 41/122=33.6%. Smokers clearly worse; largest absolute gap in the middle strata.
data.Name: appleton-1996-55-64

data.Description: Age 65-74 at baseline. Smokers: 31/37=83.8% mortality. Non-smokers: 101/129=78.3%. Smokers worse. Small smoker group (37) — most heavy smokers in this cohort had already died before reaching 65 at baseline (survivorship selection).
data.Name: appleton-1996-65-74

data.Description: Age 75+ at baseline. Smokers: 13/13=100% mortality. Non-smokers: 64/64=100%. Both groups reach 100% at 20-year follow-up — everyone in this stratum died. StratumGap=0. Heavy survivorship selection: only 13 smokers survived to 75+ at baseline vs 64 non-smokers.
data.Name: appleton-1996-75plus

data.Description: Age under 50. Vaccinated CFR: 21/89,807=0.023%. Unvaccinated CFR: 48/147,612=0.033%. Vaccinated clearly better. Low absolute mortality in both groups — under-50 COVID Delta deaths are rare regardless of vaccination status.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-under-50

data.Description: Age 50 and over. Vaccinated CFR: 460/27,307=1.68%. Unvaccinated CFR: 205/3,440=5.96%. Vaccinated dramatically better (3.5× lower death rate). The enormous relative risk reduction in this high-mortality stratum is the true vaccine signal — but it is hidden in the pooled comparison because so few unvaccinated people are in this stratum (3,440 vs 27,307 vaccinated).
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-50plus

data.Description: High-risk CABG patients (EuroSCORE-equivalent ≥5). High-volume hospitals (A) are disproportionately assigned these harder cases — 40% of A’s caseload vs 25% of B’s. A wins this stratum (95.0% vs 92.0%). The over-representation of hard cases in A drags A’s pooled rate below the equal-weight average.
data.Name: hannan-1994-high-risk

data.Description: Low-risk CABG patients (EuroSCORE-equivalent <5). Low-volume hospitals (B) have proportionally more of these easier cases — 75% of B’s caseload vs 60% of A’s. A still wins this stratum (97.8% vs 96.0%). The allocation imbalance compresses A’s pooled advantage without reversing it.
data.Name: hannan-1994-low-risk

CaseCells.Description: Table: CaseCells — the raw leaves of the DAG. One row per (study, stratum, treatment) combination recording the aggregate count of successes and total cases. Every derived fact in the model traces back to these numbers. Nothing above this row is a raw input.

schema.name: CaseCellId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: The stratum this cell belongs to (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: The treatment applied in this cell (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: Successes
schema.Description: Number of patients in this cell who had a successful outcome. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: Cases
schema.Description: Total number of patients in this cell. RAW INPUT — never derived.

schema.name: CellSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this cell: Successes / Cases. The first derived fact in the DAG.

schema.name: TotalCasesForTreatment
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata for this treatment in this study — the denominator for TreatmentExposureFraction.

schema.name: TreatmentExposureFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of this treatment's total cases that fall in this stratum: Cases / TotalCasesForTreatment. High imbalance across strata is the mechanism of confounding — when treatments are allocated very differently across strata, the pooled rate conflates treatment effect with stratum difficulty.

StratumSummaries.Description: Table: StratumSummaries — one row per (study, stratum, treatment). Computes the success rate WITHIN each stratum for each treatment. This is the stratified view that contradicts the pooled conclusion when a Simpson's Paradox reversal is present. Each row belongs to both a Stratum and a Treatment in the same Study.

schema.name: StratumSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-small-A).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: StratumLabel
schema.Description: Which stratum (matches Strata.StratumLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentLabel
schema.Description: Which treatment (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: StratumSuccesses
schema.Description: Total successes in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this (stratum, treatment) cell for this study.

schema.name: StratumSuccessRate
schema.Description: Success rate within this stratum for this treatment: StratumSuccesses / StratumCases. The true per-stratum picture.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesA
schema.Description: Successes for treatment A in this (study, stratum) — used to determine the per-stratum winner.

schema.name: StratumCasesA
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateA
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment A in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumSuccessesB
schema.Description: Successes for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumCasesB
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this (study, stratum).

schema.name: StratumRateB
schema.Description: Success rate for treatment B in this stratum.

schema.name: StratumWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins in this stratum: 'A' if StratumRateA > StratumRateB, else 'B'. Used by TreatmentRankings to detect paradox.

schema.name: StudyTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases across ALL strata and ALL treatments in this study. The denominator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases in this stratum across both treatments (A + B combined). Numerator for StratumFraction.

schema.name: StratumFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of the study's total cases that fall in this stratum: StratumTotalCases / StudyTotalCases. The weight the confounder assigns to each stratum. A large StratumFraction on a low-success stratum pulls the pooled rate down; when that stratum is also over-allocated to one treatment, it creates the reversal.

schema.name: WeightedStratumRate
schema.Description: StratumFraction × StratumSuccessRate. The contribution of this (stratum, treatment) row to the study-level pooled rate. Summing WeightedStratumRate across all strata for a given treatment reconstructs that treatment's pooled rate from stratum weights — witnessing the mechanism equation.

schema.name: TreatmentACasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment A in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentA's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentBCasesHere
schema.Description: Cases for treatment B in this stratum — used to compute TreatmentB's allocation fraction in this stratum.

schema.name: TreatmentATotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment A across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionA.

schema.name: TreatmentBTotalCases
schema.Description: Total cases for treatment B across all strata in this study — denominator for AllocationFractionB.

schema.name: AllocationFractionA
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment A's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentACasesHere / TreatmentATotalCases. Equivalent to TreatmentExposureFraction for A.

schema.name: AllocationFractionB
schema.Description: Fraction of treatment B's total cases that land in this stratum: TreatmentBCasesHere / TreatmentBTotalCases.

schema.name: AllocationBias
schema.Description: AllocationFractionA minus AllocationFractionB: how much more of treatment A (relative to B) is concentrated in this stratum. Positive = A is over-allocated here; negative = B is over-allocated here. When AllocationBias is large and negative in a low-success stratum, treatment A is under-represented in the hard cases — making A look worse in the pooled view than it is in any stratum. This is the confounder's fingerprint as a signed number.

schema.name: StratumGap
schema.Description: Signed rate difference within this stratum: StratumRateA minus StratumRateB. Positive = A wins here; negative = B wins here; zero = tied. When every stratum has StratumGap > 0 but the pooled gap is negative, the contradiction IS Simpson's Paradox made arithmetically visible in one column. Multiply StratumGap × StratumFraction and sum across strata to get the equal-weight pooled gap (the signal allocation would produce without confounding).

schema.name: WeightedStratumGap
schema.Description: StratumGap × StratumFraction: the contribution of this stratum's treatment-rate difference to the equal-weight pooled gap. Summing WeightedStratumGap across all TreatmentA rows for a study gives WeightedStratumGapSum — the pooled gap the study would show under balanced allocation. This is the per-stratum building block of the sign-flip test.

ModelSummary.Description: Table: ModelSummary — one row describing the epistemic coverage of this entire model instance. Aggregates across all TreatmentRankings to witness how many studies were examined, how many showed a full reversal, how many were explained, and the average paradox strength. The model holds a mirror to itself.

schema.name: ModelSummaryId
schema.Description: Unique identifier for this model instance.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=TRUE (strict full reversal).

schema.name: NonReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: StudyCount
schema.Description: Total number of TreatmentRankings in this model: ReversalCount + NonReversalCount.

schema.name: ExplainedCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsParadoxExplained=TRUE (reversal AND confirmed confounder).

schema.name: ZeroStrengthCount
schema.Description: Studies with StrataWonByLoser=0: no strata go against the pooled winner. These are structurally paradox-free.

schema.name: PartialCount
schema.Description: Studies with ParadoxStrength > 0 but IsReversal=FALSE: partial paradoxes real but not formally complete.

schema.name: TotalParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Sum of ParadoxStrength across all studies. Used to compute average.

schema.name: AvgParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Average ParadoxStrength across all studies: TotalParadoxStrength / StudyCount. A scalar summary of how paradox-rich this dataset is.

schema.name: TypeACount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-A: sign flip AND full reversal (ReversalIntensity=1). The canonical Simpson's Paradox — allocation distortion reverses the pooled winner.

schema.name: TypeBCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-B: sign flip but partial reversal (ReversalIntensity<1). Allocation distortion creates a sign flip but the pooled signal partially reflects per-stratum reality.

schema.name: TypeCCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-C: no sign flip but substantial allocation distortion (>0.01). The pooled winner is correct but the pooled magnitude is distorted by allocation imbalance.

schema.name: TypeDCount
schema.Description: Studies classified as Type-D: no sign flip, negligible distortion. Pooled analysis is trustworthy — allocation is sufficiently balanced.

schema.name: TypeAFraction
schema.Description: Fraction of studies that are Type-A (full canonical reversals). TypeACount / StudyCount.

schema.name: DistortionTaxonomyCoverage
schema.Description: Human-readable summary of the four-type distribution: e.g. 'A:2 B:1 C:1 D:0'. The model's self-portrait of its own distortion geometry.

schema.name: ReversalCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal_v2=TRUE (sign-flip definition). Includes both Type-A (strict/unanimous) and Type-B (extended/partial sign-flip). This is the primary reversal count under the v2 definition; ReversalCount (unanimity) is now the Type-A subtype.

schema.name: NonReversalCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsReversal_v2=FALSE (no sign flip under the v2 definition). Covers Type-C and Type-D only — studies where the equal-weight and actual pooled signals point in the same direction.

schema.name: ExtendedReversalCount
schema.Description: Number of studies that are reversals under the v2 definition but not under the unanimity criterion: ReversalCountV2 minus ReversalCount. These are the Type-B studies — the ones the old definition missed. In the current dataset: 2 (berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996).

schema.name: ExplainedCountV2
schema.Description: Number of studies with IsParadoxExplained_v2=TRUE: sign-flip reversal AND confirmed confounder. Includes the Type-B studies that IsParadoxExplained missed. The gap between ExplainedCountV2 and ExplainedCount is the number of extended reversals with causal accounts.

schema.name: DistortionOnlyCount
schema.Description: Number of studies with real allocation distortion but no sign flip: TypeCCount. The pooled winner is correct but the effect size is biased. Distinct from ZeroStrengthCount, which conflates Type-C (real distortion) with Type-D (neutral).

StratumVariables.Description: Table: StratumVariables — one row per study, naming the variable that defines the strata and describing its causal role. A confounder affects both treatment assignment AND outcome; a mediator is downstream of treatment; a contested variable is one where the causal role is disputed. IsConfounder is derived from these two boolean flags.

schema.name: StratumVariableId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-stone-size).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: VariableName
schema.Description: The name of the stratifying variable (e.g. stone_size, department, age_group).

schema.name: CausalRole
schema.Description: The variable's causal role: 'confounder' (affects both assignment and outcome), 'mediator' (downstream of treatment), 'contested' (causal role disputed), 'unknown'.

schema.name: AffectsTreatmentAssignment
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences which treatment a patient receives. Required for confounding.

schema.name: AffectsOutcome
schema.Description: TRUE if this variable causally influences the outcome independently of treatment. Required for confounding.

schema.name: IsConfounder
schema.Description: TRUE when AffectsTreatmentAssignment AND AffectsOutcome AND CausalRole = 'confounder'. A confounder is the classic driver of Simpson's Paradox.

schema.name: MechanismNote
schema.Description: Plain-language description of how this variable creates the confounding: why it affects both assignment and outcome.

data.MechanismNote: Large kidney stones were preferentially assigned to Treatment A (the more aggressive procedure), while small stones more often received Treatment B. Independently, large stones have lower baseline cure rates regardless of treatment. This dual influence — on both assignment and on outcome — is what drives the pooled reversal.
data.Name: kidney-1986-stone-size

data.MechanismNote: Department choice correlates with both gender (women applied disproportionately to competitive departments) and admission rate (competitive departments admit fewer applicants regardless of gender). However, department choice may itself be downstream of gender — if women systematically self-select into harder departments due to societal factors, then department is a mediator, not a confounder. The causal role is genuinely contested in the literature. IsConfounder=FALSE because CausalRole='contested', not because the flags are false.
data.Name: berkeley-1973-department

data.MechanismNote: In this counterfactual, stone size still affects outcome (hard cases have lower success rates) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — both treatments receive exactly 50% of their cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is now independent of stone size, no confounding occurs and AllocationBias=0 everywhere. IsConfounder=FALSE (AffectsTreatmentAssignment=false). The reversal is impossible by construction.
data.Name: kidney-balanced-stone-size

data.MechanismNote: The stratum variable affects outcome (easy vs hard conditions) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — each treatment receives exactly 50% of its cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is independent of stratum, no confounding can occur and the pooled rate must agree with every per-stratum comparison.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Case difficulty affects both which treatment patients receive (A is over-allocated to hard cases: 60% vs 40%) and the outcome (hard cases have lower success rates for both treatments). This IS confounding — but the confounding is not strong enough to flip the pooled winner. A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled. The allocation compresses A's pooled advantage (from equal-weight 10pp down to actual 6pp) but does not erase it. This is type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Hospital baseline UTI incidence affects both whether a patient received prophylaxis (high-incidence hospitals were more likely to forgo it, concentrating controls there) and the outcome (high-incidence hospitals have higher UTI rates regardless of treatment). Most prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals (1113/1279=87%), which depresses the aggregate prophylaxis UTI rate below the aggregate control rate — inverting the within-stratum finding.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-hospital-incidence

data.MechanismNote: Victim race affects both defendant race (cross-race homicides are rare — most black defendants killed black victims, most white defendants killed white victims) and sentencing outcome (white-victim cases receive the death penalty ~5× more often: ~17.7% vs ~3.3%). This is classic confounding. Black defendants are disproportionately concentrated in the black-victim stratum (low sentence rate), pulling their aggregate death penalty rate below white defendants' rate — even though black defendants are MORE likely to be sentenced to death within each victim-race stratum.
data.Name: radelet-1981-victim-race

data.MechanismNote: The season year affects both the player's at-bat count (treatment assignment proxy — Justice's heaviest season was 1995 when his average was lowest; Jeter's heaviest were 1996-97 when his average was higher) and the outcome (players hit differently in different years). Justice front-loaded his ABs into his worst year (411 of 1046 = 39% in 1995 at .253); Jeter back-loaded into his stronger years (1236 of 1284 = 96% in 1996-97 at .300-.314). This differential allocation of AB-weight across years flips the combined average despite Justice winning every year individually.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-season

data.MechanismNote: Age at baseline confounds both smoking status and 20-year mortality. Smoking affects age distribution in the cohort (survivorship bias: people who smoked heavily and were older had already died before the 1972-74 baseline survey, so the surviving smokers skew younger). Age strongly determines 20-year mortality regardless of smoking. The result: smokers are over-represented in low-mortality age strata (18-44), pulling the pooled smoker mortality rate below the true within-stratum risk. This is the textbook epidemiological confounding by age pattern, often called the "healthy smoker" fallacy in this particular dataset.
data.Name: appleton-1996-age

data.MechanismNote: Age group is a powerful confounder. It affects vaccine uptake (older people were prioritised and had much higher vaccination rates in the UK's 2021 rollout — 23% of vaccinated are 50+ vs only 2.3% of unvaccinated) and strongly determines COVID-19 mortality (50+ CFR is ~40× higher than under-50 CFR in this dataset regardless of vaccination). The vaccinated cohort is therefore loaded with high-risk patients, inflating the pooled vaccinated death rate to 0.41% vs 0.17% for unvaccinated — reversing the within-stratum relationship where vaccines reduce death rates by 3× to 4× in every age group.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-age

data.MechanismNote: Patient risk class affects both hospital assignment (high-volume centers receive referrals of sicker patients — 40% of A caseload is high-risk vs 25% at B) and surgical outcome (high-risk patients have higher operative mortality regardless of hospital volume). High-volume hospitals win in every risk stratum and also win pooled, but their pooled survival advantage is compressed from 2.4pp (equal-weight stratum average) down to 1.7pp (actual pooled) because they carry more hard cases. IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion>0 — the geometry of type-C distortion.
data.Name: hannan-1994-patient-risk

TreatmentRankings.Description: Table: TreatmentRankings — one row per treatment-pair comparison within a study. Derives the pooled winner, the per-stratum winner, and the IsReversal boolean. The paradox emerges here as a derived fact: IsReversal is true when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree.

schema.name: TreatmentRankingId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A-vs-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentA
schema.Description: Label for the first treatment in the comparison (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentB
schema.Description: Label for the second treatment in the comparison.

schema.name: TotalCasesA
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentA across all strata in this study.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesA
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentA across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentA: TotalSuccessesA / TotalCasesA.

schema.name: TotalCasesB
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesB
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentB: TotalSuccessesB / TotalCasesB.

schema.name: PooledWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins when strata are ignored: the one with the higher pooled rate.

schema.name: StratumCount
schema.Description: Number of strata in this study. Counted from StratumSummaries filtered to TreatmentA rows (one row per stratum).

schema.name: StrataWonByA
schema.Description: Number of strata where A wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentA. These are the one-per-stratum sentinel rows.

schema.name: StrataWonByB
schema.Description: Number of strata where B wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentB.

schema.name: PerStratumWinner
schema.Description: The treatment that wins in every stratum — 'A' if StrataWonByA = StratumCount, 'B' if StrataWonByB = StratumCount, else 'none' (no unanimous per-stratum winner).

schema.name: IsReversal
schema.Description: TRUE when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree — i.e. Simpson's Paradox is present. FALSE otherwise. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.

schema.name: ConfoundersInStudy
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose IsConfounder = TRUE. When > 0, the paradox has a causal explanation.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The model witnesses its own explanatory completeness — or its limits.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained_v2
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal_v2 is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The v2 counterpart to IsParadoxExplained — captures berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996, which have confirmed confounders but do not satisfy the unanimity criterion. Together with IsParadoxExplained, this field witnesses exactly which studies the old definition left unexplained despite having a causal account.

schema.name: PooledGap
schema.Description: Absolute difference between the two pooled rates: |PooledRateA - PooledRateB|. The size of the aggregate misleading signal.

schema.name: StrataWonByLoser
schema.Description: Number of strata won by the pooled loser — the counter-signal. For full reversals this equals StratumCount; for partial paradoxes it is between 0 and StratumCount.

schema.name: ParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Scalar severity of the paradox: PooledGap × (StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount). Zero when no strata go against the pooled winner. Positive for partial paradoxes. Maximum when every stratum contradicts the pooled result.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsA
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentA reconstructed as a weighted average of stratum rates: SUM(WeightedStratumRate) across all stratum rows for TreatmentA. Must match PooledRateA — this witnesses the mechanism equation: the pooled rate IS a weighted average of stratum rates, weighted by StratumFraction.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsB
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentB reconstructed as a weighted average. Must match PooledRateB. If it does, the mechanism equation is verified: reversal is purely a consequence of differential weighting.

schema.name: ReversalIntensity
schema.Description: StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount: the fraction of strata that go against the pooled winner. Zero for no paradox, 1.0 for full reversal, between 0 and 1 for partial. This is the allocation-side measure of how deeply the confounding has penetrated the stratified view.

schema.name: ThresholdMargin
schema.Description: ReversalIntensity minus 0.5: positive when more than half the strata go against the pooled winner (reversal is robust), zero at the tipping point (exactly half), negative when fewer than half oppose the pooled winner (reversal is fragile or absent). A study with ThresholdMargin > 0 is robustly paradoxical; one with ThresholdMargin < 0 has a weak or absent paradox.

schema.name: SignedPooledGap
schema.Description: Signed difference between pooled rates: PooledRateA minus PooledRateB. Positive = A wins pooled; negative = B wins pooled. Unlike PooledGap (which is absolute), this preserves direction — essential for detecting sign flips between the equal-weight and actual pooled signals.

schema.name: WeightedStratumGapSum
schema.Description: Sum of WeightedStratumGap (= StratumGap × StratumFraction) across all StratumSummaries rows where TreatmentLabel = TreatmentA for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap the pooled analysis would produce if allocation were perfectly balanced across strata. When this has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not merely compressed the signal: it has flipped it entirely.

schema.name: IsSignFlip
schema.Description: TRUE when WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs — the allocation has reversed the direction of the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show. This is the continuous generalisation of IsReversal: it does not require every stratum to agree, only that the equal-weight estimate and the actual pooled estimate point in opposite directions. Berkeley 1973 satisfies IsSignFlip=TRUE despite IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: AllocationDistortion
schema.Description: The magnitude of how far the allocation has bent the pooled signal: |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Zero means the allocation is neutral — the pooled analysis faithfully represents the equal-weight stratum evidence. A large AllocationDistortion means the allocation is doing most of the work: the pooled number is mostly noise from how cases were assigned, not signal about which treatment is better. This measure does NOT require a sign flip to be nonzero — it captures any allocation-induced distortion, not just reversals.

schema.name: DistortionType
schema.Description: Geometric classification of this study's allocation-induced distortion. Four exhaustive types: A = full sign-flip with unanimous per-stratum reversal (IsSignFlip=TRUE AND ReversalIntensity=1.0); B = sign-flip but not unanimous (IsSignFlip=TRUE AND ReversalIntensity<1.0); C = distortion without sign flip (IsSignFlip=FALSE AND AllocationDistortion>0.01 — the allocation has compressed or inflated the pooled margin but not reversed it); D = effectively neutral (AllocationDistortion≤0.01 — pooled analysis is trustworthy with respect to this stratification). The taxonomy is derived purely from the geometric coordinates; it does not depend on causal attribution.

schema.name: PolicyImplication
schema.Description: The researcher action implied by the geometric classification. Derived from DistortionType: type A (full reversal) → 'stratify-immediately' — the pooled conclusion is directionally wrong; do not act on it without stratifying. Type B (partial sign-flip) → 'investigate-confounder' — the equal-weight signal opposes the pooled signal, but the per-stratum picture is mixed; the causal mechanism needs examination before trusting either estimate. Type C (compression without flip) → 'check-allocation-bias' — the pooled winner is probably correct but the effect size is distorted; report adjusted or standardized margins rather than the raw pooled gap. Type D (neutral) → 'pooled-analysis-trustworthy' — allocation is not materially distorting the signal; the pooled conclusion is safe with respect to this stratification.

schema.name: IsReversal_v2
schema.Description: The sign-flip definition of reversal: TRUE when the equal-weight pooled signal (WeightedStratumGapSum) and the actual pooled signal (SignedPooledGap) point in opposite directions. Strictly generalizes the unanimity criterion — every Type-A study satisfies both IsReversal and IsReversal_v2, but Berkeley-1973 and Appleton-1996 satisfy IsReversal_v2 without satisfying IsReversal. The unanimity criterion (IsReversal) is now understood as the Type-A subtype of this broader definition.

schema.name: DefinitionDelta
